data.epi_content:
I thought Vivian looked beautiful even when crying.

When her emotions were slightly heightened like this, I wondered if I could cast a tiny spell to crush that small head of hers.

No, that wouldn't work. Even if Vivian let her guard down, there's no way I could kill her.

Anyway, when Evan told me why he had such profound meaning to me, my face must have turned slightly red without me realizing it, as my cheeks felt hot when I touched them.

Of course I couldn't help but be moved.

Death wasn't the end—somehow he had gone back through my words, concluded I had traveled back in time, and done something crazy.

I probably would have been moved even if it had been Ivan lying next to me instead of Evan.

No, that's going too far.

In a way, it might just be Evan's form of escapism, but that's not so bad either.

After all, he fled toward me, someone he thought had died.

"Vivian, I can't kill you. Neither can Evan.

Even if we came back to kill you, some twist of fate would probably intervene.

So I don't care if you get fucked by some black guy or some ice-eating whore."

What an absurd human being.

His very existence is nonsensical.

He's killed tens of thousands, yet their lives are worth less than Vivian's.

Vivian always speaks of fairness and justice, yet fails to realize she stands at the pinnacle of the greatest absurdity.

"Well, emotionally charged old stories are boring."

I took a sweet potato from my pocket and crunched into it.

Perhaps because some time had passed, it tasted slightly sweet.

"So let's stop facing each other and exchanging nonsense like this."

"...Nonsense?"

Vivian apparently didn't think her words were nonsense.

I was just spewing whatever came to mind based on emotion, while you, Vivian, weren't speaking your own thoughts but borrowing the moral platitudes of others.

In a way, it's like a patchwork. Nothing of your own, just other people's ideas stitched together—anyone can see it's not really yours.

Not my problem.

"Just let Evan and me go. Then at least we'll stop this dog's work of traveling around cutting off people's heads."

"...How can I trust you?"

"Why, it's hard to believe I can travel back in time too.

Even when you see it with your own eyes and confirm there's no manipulation in the magic."

Ivan wagged his tail beside me and gently bit my hand.

Not hard, just enough to tickle a little.

He must be a bit bored or hungry.

"Believe me, and good fortune will come! Ha! Not funny?"

Vivian pressed her brow hard, as if trying to hide her grimacing expression.

I spoke teasingly with a slight smile, making her already throbbing head throb even more.

"Otherwise I'll just have to escape.

Well, I might lose a leg or arm while escaping, or if I'm unlucky, my head might fly off, but if I survive, I'll just have to go around burning things here and there again."

As I spoke with a snicker, Vivian broke into a slight cold sweat.

"Vivian, how many will die? You can't catch me.

I might kill as many as Evan did, not just tens of thousands.

In the end, not a blade of grass will remain in this Empire."

"How can you say that..."

"If my family were still around, I might hesitate, but the territory already belongs to the Imperial family.

They're not my territory's people but Imperial subjects, so I wouldn't feel any qualms about killing them."

Well, Evan also diligently buried the knights sent by his family.
data.epi_content2:
At those words, Vivian bit her lip.

Then she sighed deeply for a long time.

Actually, this conversation wasn't even necessary.

If she had the upper hand, she wouldn't have come looking for me.

The moment Vivian came to find me, she essentially admitted her defeat.

As Evan once told me, Vivian sincerely cares more about saving ten nameless people than one precious person.

What good is strength?

In the end, there's no way to keep me captive.

Evan could escape from the capital if he wanted to.

He probably just stayed put because he gained quite a lot there.

Someday they might be able to kill Evan and me.

But how many people will die until then?

I extend my hand to Vivian and say:

"Vivian, as I said earlier, let's compromise.

How can you do anything against someone who can infinitely turn back time?

You might survive, but those around you won't. Not everyone is as exceptional as you."

At those words, Vivian took my hand, and after keeping her mouth shut for a while, she began speaking again just as the tobacco I was holding had burned down completely.

"So it's right to stop killing anyone and just go somewhere else.

No more of this—picking on the weak and running away when knights or mages are sent."

"Yes."

"You're saying I should let go of a vile and despicable mass murderer who will go down in history, right before my eyes."

"Yes."

"Someone who practices black magic, making even oaths ineffective."

Vivian gives a slightly bitter smile.

Judging by the blood trickling from the right side of her mouth, she seems to have made up her mind.

"But do you have a choice?

To farm, you need to settle on land.

You have to till the soil, sow seeds, and harvest.

You take that as taxes, and then you build something to protect them and send people.

Unless you're going to ride horses and herd cattle.

In the end, you're tied to the land, but we're not."

To emphasize my point, I grabbed one of the skulls that had been rattling its jaw and crushed it underfoot.

As soon as I finished speaking, there was a cracking sound as the skull shattered.

"So what will you do?"

I was still extending my hand to Vivian. She closed her eyes once, then accepted my hand and shook it.

"...How exactly do you want to be released?"

And she asks the most important question.

"Well, this is quite kind of you.

Just let it be known that Evan died. The crippled witch, unable to bear the grief after the black mage's death, also died.

Ah, right. I need to give this to Evan for things to be resolved."

Thinking Vivian might accept, I handed her the letter I had written for Evan.

There's nothing special in it.

Just instructions to pretend to fight, take Vivian's magic hit, and then escape through the underground waterway.

On the night of the full moon.

Several days passed after shaking hands with Vivian.

I don't know if she made her decision based on emotion, or if she fell for my suggestion that she bears some responsibility for what happened to me since she essentially killed me too, but this seems too good to be true.
data.epi_content3:
Thinking about it coldly, it feels like I might have fallen into a trap.

Vivian isn't stupid—there's no way she would just let Evan and me go.

We probably both know this.

As I said from the beginning, the time for resolution through dialogue has long passed.

Yes, it's a trap.

That bitter expression is the trademark of young dreamers imagining themselves making sacrifices for their country.

But what does it matter? I need to at least help Evan escape.

Who knows, maybe Evan has prepared something.

Even if I have to give up half my body and be called the "upper body ghost" instead of the crippled witch, I dragged out the conversation and clashed emotionally to try to escape somehow, and she gave in so easily. I'll meet Evan too, so what's the problem?

When we meet, we'll find a way to escape.

Occasionally, the cursed words full of resentment that those I killed with my own hands—not using skeletons, but personally smashing their heads and hacking at them with weapons—uttered before their deaths would echo in my ears, but I'm used to it.

Reading Vivian's story while trapped in that tiny room was all to escape from such voices.

At this point, a few more don't make much difference.

I head toward the cliff where Ivan and I used to sit and watch the tedious siege of the capital.

The soldiers surrounding the castle are still dying and killing around the walls... though skeletons don't actually die.

I think I understand a bit why Vivian wants to give up.

"It's about time."

Though fewer than what Evan controls, I led my rattling, walking skeletons to the underground waterway.

Usually, soldiers would be guarding it, but due to unavoidable pressure—Vivian's orders—they had long been driven back.

Vivian and some self-important humans would probably appear here with weapons, and I would likely die.

As I entered the capital, I saw someone covered in rags.

"Evan."

"Erica, I think we need to escape."

"I know. There's no way Vivian would just let us go."

"I came out knowing I was being tricked, and it really was from you."

"Yes."

I was about to embrace him after such a long time apart, but remembering the blood covering our bodies and our dirty clothes, I kept my distance.

And to mask the smell, I took out some tobacco from my pocket and handed it to Evan, who accepted it with a smile without saying anything.

"Don't worry. Vivian will let us go."

"Before that, Evan, stick out your right foot in front of me."

I limped away from Ivan, got up, kicked Evan in the shin, and fell backward.

"There, done."

Evan tilted his head, confused about what just happened.

"That's punishment for not coming back after a year."

"My goodness."

Evan helped me up after I fell on my backside.

"Erica, our two lives versus the 300,000 hostages in here starving to death. What do you think Vivian will choose?"

"I don't know, and I don't want to know."

We held hands and began to make our way out through the waterway.

As expected, Vivian wasn't there, but several men were blocking the path.
